content:
Internal platforms create winners and silent tax payers.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Platform team shipped @company/auth-client and the announcement was cheerful. One library. One OAuth flow. One place to fix bugs. Two product teams estimated a week saved each on the next quarter's roadmap.
Six months later, four other teams lost a month collectively on version skew, mystery defaults, and upgrade windows that never aligned with their launches.
The shared library saved two teams a week. It cost everyone else a month. Internal platforms create winners and silent tax payers. Finance rarely lines up both sides on the same spreadsheet.
The Visible Win and the Invisible Invoice
Winners:
→ Teams whose roadmap matched the platform release train → Teams with staff engineers who joined the working group → Teams whose use case matched the happy path encoded in v1
Tax payers:
→ Teams on older runtime constraints waiting for compatibility → Teams needing a edge grant type platform deferred as “not common” → Teams who imported the library for one function and inherited twelve transitive behaviors → Teams whose on-call inherited failures in a dependency they do not control
The library worked. The economics were lopsided.
How the Month Disappeared
Concrete costs I watched happen:
→ Three services pinned different minor versions because changelog communication was Slack threads → Security patch required coordinated deploy across seven repos; two teams missed the window and got emergency escalations → Debug session where stack traces disappeared into wrapper code nobody on the calling team had read → Feature request closed as “use the extension hook” that existed only in a doc example, not in prod → Holiday freeze blocked platform release; product team forked internally “temporarily”
Forks are how tax payers stop paying until the fork becomes the real platform and someone writes a Medium post about fragmentation.
Money Voice: Price the Platform Like a Product
If platform work saves time, it also spends time. Track both.
Questions finance understands:
→ What is upgrade latency from CVE publish to last consumer deployed? → How many engineer-weeks spent on migration support last quarter? → What percentage of incidents touch shared libs versus service code? → Which teams opt out and what do they build instead?
Without those numbers, platform teams report adoption metrics. Adoption is not value. Adoption can be coercion.
We started publishing an internal “platform tax” estimate alongside “platform savings.” Uncomfortable meetings. Better allocations.
When Shared Libraries Are Worth the Tax
Libraries win when:
→ The problem is genuinely identical across teams with low variation → Versioning and release discipline match consumer need, not platform convenience → Support SLAs exist with on-call rotation platform pays for → Escape hatches are documented without shame → Deprecation dates are real, not aspirational
Libraries lose when they are personality wrappers around “we prefer this style” or when platform throughput is measured by repo count instead of reduced incident rate.
Two teams saving a week each is ten person-days saved. Six teams losing a week each is thirty person-days burned. Net negative with good slide graphics.
Designing for Silent Tax Payers
Practices that reduced our burn:
→ Semantic versioning enforced by CI, not convention → Consumer contract tests platform runs before release → Clear ownership: every wrapper has a named team on-call, not a mailing list → Roadmap input from non-platform teams with veto on breaking releases during freeze windows → Sunset policy for forks — merge upstream or document why divergence persists
Also: money talks. Fund platform support headcount from the savings claims. If you cannot staff support, you cannot staff adoption.
The Political Problem Is Allocation, Not Code
Engineers debate abstraction quality. Executives debate budgets. Silent tax payers do not complain in roadmap reviews because complaining sounds anti-collaboration.
Give them a line item. “Migration support” visible. “Version skew incidents” visible. Then collaboration stops being a moral argument and becomes a portfolio decision.
Shared libraries are not free speed. They are centralized speed with distributed friction. Price the friction or resent the platform team unfairly.
A Fork Story That Taught Finance More Than a Deck.
One tax payer team forked @company/auth-client after a breaking release landed during their launch freeze. Platform called it fragmentation. The fork team called it survival.
The fork lived four months. It duplicated bug fixes twice. It missed one security patch by a week. The cost was measurable: nine engineer-days plus one short incident when the fork drifted on token refresh semantics.
We presented both sides in a portfolio review without moralizing. Savings from the two winner teams: ten person-days per quarter. Tax from fork plus skew plus emergency patches: twenty-two person-days over the same window.
Leadership approved dedicated platform support headcount the next month. Not because the library was bad. Because the spreadsheet finally showed both sides.
Money voice again: if you cannot show tax payers on the same slide as winners, you are running a subsidy you cannot defend when budgets tighten.
Contracts That Reduced Surprise Upgrades.
After the fork episode we treated the library like an external vendor:
→ Published upgrade windows with consumer opt-in, not surprise Friday releases → Breaking changes required migration guide and office hours, not only changelog entries → Consumer repos ran contract tests in CI that platform could see failing before tag → Major versions supported in parallel for ninety days minimum → Explicit “supported consumer” list with named liaison per product team
Adoption went down slightly. Incidents from version skew went down more. Net happiness up if you believe on-call engineers are people.
Platform teams want velocity. Product teams want predictability. Finance wants both numbers on one page. Shared libraries sit at that triangle unless you price the friction honestly.
When to Say No to a Shared Abstraction.
We now ask five questions before greenlighting a shared library instead of copy-paste:
→ Do at least three teams share identical semantics, not similar vibes? → Is the failure domain acceptable to centralize? → Can platform commit to support SLAs with named on-call? → Is there a credible escape hatch for edge consumers? → Will finance fund both savings and support, not only savings slides?
Two nos is a local module. Five yeses is a platform bet. Everything between is where silent tax payers come from.
Money voice because budgets are stories: winners get roadmap credit, tax payers pay in invisible hours until someone puts them on a spreadsheet.
Before you adopt the next internal SDK, ask who maintains it at 3 A.M. and who gains a week on the roadmap slide. If those answers diverge, negotiate funding before you negotiate API shape.
Internal platforms create winners and silent tax payers. Your job is to make the tax visible early enough that resentment does not become architecture.
Platform ROI slides that show only saved weeks are marketing. Slides that show saved weeks and spent months are engineering leadership. Finance trusts the second kind.
The shared library that wins long term is the one whose tax payers would choose it again because support showed up, not because escape was impossible.
Count the month you lose across three teams, not only the week two teams save. That arithmetic belongs in every platform pitch deck, not only in retrospectives after frustration boils over.
Two teams saved a week. Four teams lost a month. Net negative. Good slide anyway, until you do fifth grade math in public.
Platform wins should survive a spreadsheet, not only applause in Slack.
Silent tax payers do not leave angry comments in roadmap docs. They leave quietly for teams with less coupling. Count that too.
A shared library without a tax line item is a subsidy. Subsidies are fine when funded. Unfunded subsidies become internal politics. Price the month, not only the week.
When you want the system design and production economics that make internal platforms survivable — not just demoable — The Production Engineering Library: The Complete Collection for Production Systems, System Design & Backend Engineering is the reference I use when platform ROI debates need adult vocabulary.
For scenario practice on tradeoffs, coupling, and backend ownership — the stuff platform slides skip — Froquiz offers 10,000+ questions across SQL, Docker, Git, AWS, Java, Python, JavaScript, React, microservices, plus Senior Dev Challenge problems that feel like real build-versus-buy arguments.
Until the tax is visible, every platform roadmap looks like pure leverage.
→ Froquiz
